narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 65.9% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of similar students. You have a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60, which is above the typical 3.21 average, along with middle-family income, full-time enrollment plans, and your background as a Black male applicant. However, your Year 1 school decile of 2 and Year 3 decile of 2 are both quite low compared to averages around 5.5 to 5.7, and your LSAT score of 28 falls well below the usual 36.7. When the model evaluated your profile, it found your Year 3 decile to be the strongest factor pushing toward a higher risk of bar failure, followed closely by your LSAT score, then your Year 1 decile. Your family income level, racial background, undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time status each played smaller roles, all nudging the prediction slightly toward failure risk as well, though none outweighed those top three academic indicators. In essence, students who typically pass the bar on their first attempt rank much higher in school deciles and score better on the LSAT, often in the top half of their classes with scores above 35. Your combination stands out as riskier because low performance in later law school years like Year 3 signals potential struggles with the kind of rigorous material that predicts bar success. That said, your strong undergrad GPA shows you have real academic potential that the model noted but couldn't fully offset the other concerns. To improve your odds moving forward, focus on boosting your law school performance right away—aim for top deciles by seeking tutoring, study groups, or time management strategies, as those early signals can be turned around with consistent effort. Retaking the LSAT for a higher score could also help in future applications, since it strongly correlates with bar prep ability. Finally, targeted bar exam prep courses have helped many students in similar situations pass despite early hurdles, so building those skills now will make you a stronger candidate next time. Keep pushing—you've already shown strengths that can lead to success with the right adjustments.
